## [00:00 - 03:01]

Welcome to Launch Essentials. I'm Paul, one of your PLF coaches. This lesson is all about technology, specifically where to start. Your Launch Essentials are the core tools and technology you need to get your launch business off the ground. And that's what this video is all about. But we'll also spend some time and look at some key considerations to think about as you start investing in tools and technology for your business. Let's get started. First, where to start? When you're first starting out, there's some basic things that you absolutely have to have set up. The first is a domain and a squeeze page. This could be your own website or via something like leadpages.net. Next, you're going to need to create a lead magnet. Now, a lead magnet is what you offer via your squeeze page in exchange for someone's email. And finally, you're going to need an email management system to communicate with your prospects. Once that's in place, you can start building your list while you work on creating your product and preparing to launch. Now, one of the big questions we always get is, should I choose an all-in-one product? Or should I choose the all-a-card approach? I want to talk about both of those and lay out some pros and cons for both sides. Let's start with the all-in-one product. Think of Kajabi as an example. There are countless pros here. Ease of use, ease and speed of implementation, everything under one roof. And my favorite is a one-stop customer support. So if anything breaks, you only have one call to make and they can help you fix it. Now, the cons are that you may not want or need all of the features. So that means that you're paying for things that you're not using and it's soaking up precious resources as you get your business off the ground. Now, let's look at the all-a-card approach. Think of a WordPress website with numerous plugins as an example. The pros here are that you have greater control and adaptability. You add features as you need them and can adapt as your business grows. The cons are that there's a higher likelihood of something breaking and it's not as easy to manage on your own. In addition to looking at an all-in-one product or an all-a-card approach, there are several considerations that you should make as you start investing in tools for your business. The first is pricing and budget. Are you ready for a big investment or are you bootstrapping your launch? What type of budget do you have set aside for technology as you get this business off the ground? The next is speed of implementation. How quickly do you want or need to get everything in place? If you have a launch goal of launching in the next 60 days, you're obviously going to need things implemented much sooner than if you are going to build your list for several months and don't plan to launch for another six months. Next, you're going to want to look at features and integrations because the reality is not all tools play nice with each other. So you're going to want to make sure that whatever features and integrations you get work well with other existing platforms that you are using.
